revision.text:
[[en:Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū]][[ja:琉球八社]][[zh:琉球八社]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}}} {{Single source|date=February 28, 2014 (Friday) 11:25 (UTC)|qq=}} {{OSM Location map|coord={{coord|26.208893|N|127.687876|E|qq=}}|fullscreen-option=1|zoom=12|float=right|width=300|height=300|caption=|title=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū (Within [[Naha]] City)|mark-coord1={{coord|26|13|14.6|N|127|40|15.9|E|qq=}}|label1=Naminoue Shrine|label-pos1=left|mark-size1=8|mark-coord2={{coord|26|13|48.6|N|127|42|50.6|E|qq=}}|label2=Sueyoshi-gū|label-pos2=bottom|mark-size2=8|mark-coord3={{coord|26|12|10|N|127|40|37.5|E|qq=}}|label3=Oki Shrine|label-pos3=right|mark-size3=8|mark-coord4={{coord|26|13|15.9|N|127|41|41.8|E|qq=}}|label4=Asato Hachimangū|label-pos4=bottom|mark-size4=8|mark-coord5={{coord|26|12|36.7|N|127|42|48.3|E|qq=}}|label5=Shikina-gū|label-pos5=right|mark-size5=8|mark-coord6={{coord|26|13|41.9|N|127|40|57.4|E|qq=}}|label6=Ameku-gū|label-pos6=left|mark-size6=8|qq=}} {{OSM Location map|coord={{coord|26.347343|N|127.833984|E|qq=}}|fullscreen-option=1|zoom=10|float=right|width=300|height=300|caption=|title=Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū (Outside Naha City)|mark-coord1={{coord|26|17|34.4|N|127|46|37.9|E|qq=}}|label1=Futenma Shrine|label-pos1=left|mark-size1=8|mark-coord2={{coord|26|27|19.1|N|127|55|18.5|E|qq=}}|label2={{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}|label-pos2=bottom|mark-size2=8|qq=}} '''Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū''' (琉球八社, Ryūkyū Hassha) refers to the eight {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} that were granted special status by the royal government of the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|qid=Q28025|lt=Ryūkyū Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}} under the "System of Eight Shrines." == Explanation == === Origin === The oldest of these shrines is thought to be {{ill|Oki Shrine|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki Shrine|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, established during the time of {{ill|Minamoto no Tametomo|en|Minamoto no Tametomo|qid=Q1339356|lt=Minamoto no Tametomo|1=Minamoto no Tametomo}}, considered the father of King {{ill|Shunten|en|Shunten|qid=Q1059918|lt=Shunten|1=Shunten}}. According to ''Studies on Ryūkyū Religious History,'' both {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai Buddhism|1=Rinzai school}} and {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}} were introduced to Ryūkyū, with Rinzai receiving more royal patronage. However, eight Shingon temples were granted stipends by the royal government. These temples were often colocated with shrines (e.g., {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} was built on a popular site of local faith, and {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}} was constructed by King Shōtoku to commemorate military victories). Collectively, these shrines came to be known as the "Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū."''Studies on Ryūkyū Religious History,'' Chapter 5, Section 1, 1965. Among these, Naminoue Shrine was regarded as the foremost, partly because {{ill|Gokoku-ji|en|Gokoku-ji|qid=Q1534481|lt=Gokoku-ji|1=Gokoku-ji}}, the head temple of Shingon Buddhism in Ryūkyū, was located within its precincts.''Studies on Ryūkyū Religious History,'' Chapter 5, Section 5, Subsection 1, 1965. The shrines primarily enshrined {{ill|Kumano Gongen|ja|熊野権現|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Gongen|qq=}}, except for Asato Hachimangū, which enshrined {{ill|Hachiman|en|Hachiman|qid=Q261637|lt=Hachiman|1=Hachiman}}. ''Studies on Ryūkyū Religious History'' theorizes that the title "Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū" was propagated as part of Shingon Buddhist efforts to promote these shrines. === Priesthood === With the exception of {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}, each shrine had a designated priest. {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} was managed by the abbot of {{ill|Kin Kannon-ji|ja|金武観音寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin Kannon-ji|qq=}}. The government provided stipends to priests and maintenance funds, although {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} did not receive financial support from the royal government, technically making the system one of seven official shrines.''Studies on Ryūkyū Religious History,'' Chapter 5, Section 2, Subsection 3, 1965. The chief priest of Naminoue Shrine oversaw all seven shrines and managed appointments and promotions, which were submitted to the royal court for approval. Priests received their ceremonial attire from the court. === Ryukyu Eight Shrines After the Annexation === During the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, the Ryukyu Islands experienced a wave of religious reforms similar to those in mainland Japan. However, these reforms diverged in nature due to the {{ill|Ryukyu Disposition|en|Ryukyu Disposition|qid=Q98090884|lt=Ryukyu Disposition|1=Ryukyu Disposition}}, which abolished the Ryukyu Kingdom and established {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|qid=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}. Unlike the widespread [[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto-Buddhist separation]] and [[Haibutsu Kishaku]] seen in mainland Japan, the Ryukyuan reforms took a different course. However, after the {{ill|Invasion of Ryukyu|en|Invasion of Ryukyu|qid=Q1140134|lt=Invasion of Ryukyu|1=Invasion of Ryukyu}} in 1609, shrines of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} lineage began to receive more favorable treatment. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000|qq=}} For example, until the 16th century, prayers at the royal court were performed by Noro organizations led by {{ill|Kikoe-ōgimi|en|Kikoe-ōgimi|qid=Q11611193|lt=Kikoe-ōgimi|1=Kikoe-ōgimi}} and Zen temples, but after the invasion of the Satsuma domain, prayers for bountiful harvests were carried out by Noro organizations, prayers for the good health of the royal family and the peace of the nation were carried out by {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} shrines such as Naminoue-gu and Futenma-gu, and royal funerals and ancestral memorial services were carried out by Zen temples. In addition, in {{ill|Shō Hō|en|Shō Hō|qid=Q1041365|lt=1625|1=Shō Hō}}, it became customary to visit the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} shrines in addition to the Zen temples and Chinese shrines as a {{ill|Shō Ken|en|Shō Ken|qid=Q1061074|lt=New|1=Shō Ken}} Year's event, and in 1644, the king's visit to Futenma-gu Shrine (Hutinma-mee) became a regular event, attracting the reverence of ordinary people as well. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000|qq=}} In 1671, the head priest of Gokoku-ji Temple, Yorimichi, requested {{ill|Shō Shōken|en|Shō Shōken|qid=Q843010|lt=Shō Shōken|1=Shō Shōken}} to convert the Rinzai sect temples Manju-ji Temple (Sueyoshi-gu Shrine), Jin'o-ji Temple (Shikina-gu Shrine), and Seigen-ji Temple (Ameku-gu Shrine) to Shingon Buddhism. {{Sfn|加治順人|2000|qq=}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021|qq=}} It is believed that through these events, the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} shrines became {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Official shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=官幣社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/official shrines|qq=}}, and {{ill|the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|8=en|10=ja|11=琉球八社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/the Eight Ryukyu Shrines|1=Eight Ryukyu Shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11573652}} were established. {{Sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|qq=}} {{Sfn|加治順人|2000|qq=}} {{Sfn|新垣裕之|2021|qq=}} {{Refn|The Ryukyu Eight Shrines are {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, {{ill|Oki-no-gū|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}, {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|sn|八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}, {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}, {{ill|Futemma-gū|lt=Futemma-gū|lt=Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futenma Shrine|qq=}}, and {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}. All of them belong to the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} lineage. However, since Kin-gū did not have a shrine building or priests and did not receive maintenance funds from the royal government, there is a theory that the other seven shrines are considered official shrines{{sfn|鳥越憲三郎|1965|pp=589-596|qq=}}{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=67-70|qq=}}{{sfn|新垣裕之|2021|pp=70-73|qq=}}。Additionally, among the seven shrines listed as major shrines in the "Ryukyu Shinto Record," {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Chojugu Shrine|ja|長寿宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chojugu Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (later {{ill|Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Yomochi and Ukishima Shrines|lt=Ukishima Shrine|ja|浮島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukishima Shrine|qq=}}) and {{ill|Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja|長楽寺天満宮|sn|天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Choraku-ji Tenmangū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which are not of the {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} lineage, are excluded from the official shrines.{{sfn|加治順人|2000|pp=70-73|qq=}}。|group=Note|qq=}} According to *Studies on the History of Ryukyuan Religion*, the Meiji religious policy aimed to suppress or transform local religions and replace them with a Japan-centered national religion. This new religious framework was designed to integrate the Ryukyuan people under the Japanese state and its ideology. In this context, the Ryukyu Eight Shrines were appropriated by the Meiji government for political purposes. The study notes that, even after Okinawa Prefecture was established in Meiji 12 ({{ill|1879|en|1879|qid=Q7783|lt=Meiji 12|1=1879}}), the Ryukyuan population's allegiance remained uncertain. Many people hoped for aid from [[:en:Qing China|Qing China]], and this sentiment persisted, particularly until the {{ill|Boxer Rebellion|en|Boxer Rebellion|qid=Q150229|lt=Boxer Rebellion|1=Boxer Rebellion}}. Faced with these challenges to governance, the government sought to use religious unity to strengthen administrative control. As part of this effort, one of the Ryukyu Eight Shrines was elevated to the [[modern shrine Ranking system]] as a kanpei-chūsha (Imperial Shrine of Medium Rank) to promote loyalty to the state and imperial family. Thus, in Meiji 22 ({{ill|1889|en|1889|qid=Q7831|lt=Meiji 22|1=1889}}), the governor of Okinawa Prefecture recommended that {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} be elevated. The government approved this proposal, citing the enshrinement of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} (a divine ancestor of the imperial family), and in Meiji 23 ({{ill|1890|en|1890|qid=Q7832|lt=Meiji 23|1=1890}}), the shrine was officially Ranked as a kanpei-shōsha (Imperial Shrine of Lesser Rank)*Studies on the History of Ryukyuan Religion*, Chapter 5, Section 5, Section 2, 1965.. Regarding the other seven shrines, plans were made to classify them as village shrines. However, as Ryukyuan shrines traditionally lacked {{ill|Ujiko|en|Ujiko|qid=Q125562175|lt=Ujiko|1=Ujiko}} organizations (local parishioners), economic limitations made village shrine classification unfeasible. Additionally, the shrines’ facilities were often inadequate. As a result, these shrines were left unranked and maintained temporarily, with the intention of improving and upgrading them to village shrine status over time. The study explains that, although the Ryukyu Eight Shrines were considered equivalent to shrines in mainland Japan, significant differences remained in their organizational structures, including the absence of ujiko organizations and differences in rituals. Furthermore, during the Ryukyu Kingdom, the royal government officially recognized sacred sites such as [[utaki]] prayer grounds, hearth shrines, and the priestesses who served them as the primary state religious institutions. These sites were deeply integrated into the faith of the local populace and could not be easily abolished. In contrast, aside from {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}}, which received government funding, the other seven shrines struggled economically and spiritually, falling into severe neglect. For example, by Shōwa 12 ({{ill|1937|en|1937|qid=Q18647|lt=Showa 12|1=1937}}), [[Ameku-gū]]’s worship hall had disappeared, and its main sanctuary had collapsed with a thunderous noise, leaving it completely overturned. Despite the lack of local faith in these shrines, the Japanese government’s decision to promote the Ryukyu Eight Shrines solely based on their superficial similarity to mainland shrines is ultimately critiqued as a failure in *Studies on the History of Ryukyuan Religion*. == List of Ryukyu Eight Shrines == {| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center" ! Shrine Name !! Associated Temple !! Shrine Image !! Location !! Shrine Rank !Original lineage{{efn|This refers to the original lineage. All are {{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} today but some were originally {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家|qq=}} and switched over later. The two that stayed {{nihongo|Zenke|禅家|qq=}} were excluded from the list|qq=}} |- | {{ill|Naminoue Shrine|en|Naminoue Shrine|qid=Q704636|lt=Naminoue Shrine|1=Naminoue Shrine}} || {{ill|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|en|Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)|qid=Q3109955|lt=Gokoku-ji|1=Gokoku-ji (Okinawa)}} || [[File:Naminoue-guu.jpg|150px|Naminoue Shrine Worship Hall]] || {{ill|Naha|en|Naha|qid=Q181966|lt=Naha|1=Naha}} Wakasa
{{coord|26|13|14.62|N|127|40|15.95|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} || {{ill|Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|lt=Kanpei-shōsha|ja|官幣小社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanpei-shōsha|qq=}} [[Beppyo Shrine]] [[Ichinomiya]] |{{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} |- | {{ill|Sueyoshi-gū|ja|末吉宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sueyoshi-gū|qq=}}|| {{ill|Henshō-ji (Okinawa)|lt=Henshō-ji|kk= (Okinawa)|ja|遍照寺 (沖縄市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Henshō-ji (Okinawa)|qq=}} || [[File:Sueyoshi-gū Haiden.JPG|150px|Sueyoshi-gū Worship Hall]] || Naha, {{ill|Shuri, Okinawa|en|Shuri, Okinawa|qid=Q608415|lt=Shuri|1=Shuri, Okinawa}} Sueyoshi Town
{{coord|26|13|48.6|N|127|42|50.6|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} || Unranked Shrine |{{nihongo|Zenke|禅家|qq=}} |- | {{ill|Oki-no-gū‎|ja|沖宮|1=Oki-no-gū‎|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|13=User:Immanuelle/Oki-no-gū‎|qq=}}|| {{ill|Rinkai-ji|ja|臨海寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rinkai-ji|qq=}} || [[File:Oki-no-gū haiden.jpeg|150px|Okinomiya Torii and Worship Hall]] || Naha, Ounoyama
{{coord|26|12|10.0|N|127|40|37.5|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} || Unranked Shrine |{{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} |- | {{ill|Asato-Hachimangū|ja|安里八幡宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato-Hachimangū|qq=}}|| {{ill|Shintoku-ji|ja|神徳寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shintoku-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || [[File:Asato-Hachimangū.JPG|150px|Asato Hachiman Shrine Worship Hall and Main Sanctuary]] || Naha, {{ill|Asato|ja|安里|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asato|1=Asato|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11451640}}
{{coord|26|13|16.0|N|127|41|41.8|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} || Unranked Shrine |{{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} |- | {{ill|Shikina-gū|ja|識名宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikina-gū|qq=}}|| {{ill|Jin'ō-ji|ja|神応寺 (那覇市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jin'ō-ji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} || [[File:識名宮拝殿 - DJBzmAcUEAAL7HT.jpg|150px|Shikina-gū Worship Hall]] || Naha, Hantagawa
{{coord|26|12|36.76|N|127|42|48.39|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} || Unranked Shrine |{{nihongo|Zenke|禅家|qq=}} |- | {{ill|Ameku-gū|ja|天久宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ameku-gū|qq=}}|| {{ill|Seigen-ji|ja|聖現寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seigen-ji|qq=}} || [[File:Ameku-gū Haiden.JPG|150px|Ameku-gū Worship Hall]] || Naha, Tomari
{{coord|26|13|41.99|N|127|40|57.46|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} || Unranked Shrine |{{nihongo|Zenke|禅家|qq=}} |- | {{ill|Futemma-gū|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futemma-gū|qq=}}|| {{ill|Jingū-ji (Ginowan)|ja|神宮寺 (宜野湾市)|lt=Jingū-ji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jingū-ji (Ginowan)|qq=}} || [[File:Futemma-gū haiden.jpeg|150px|Futenma Shrine Worship Hall]] || {{ill|Ginowan, Okinawa|en|Ginowan, Okinawa|qid=Q592065|lt=Ginowan|1=Ginowan, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Futenma|ja|普天間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futenma|1=Futenma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11514557}}
{{coord|26|17|34.5|N|127|46|38|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} || Unranked Shrine |{{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} |- | {{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}}|| {{ill|Kin Kannon-ji|ja|金武観音寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin Kannon-ji|qq=}} || [[File:Kin-gū.JPG|150px|{{ill|Kin-gū|ja|金武宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kin-gū|qq=}} Sanctuary]] || {{ill|Kunigami District, Okinawa|en|Kunigami District, Okinawa|qid=Q1330132|lt=Kunigami District|1=Kunigami District, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Kin, Okinawa|en|Kin, Okinawa|qid=Q1350738|lt=Kin|1=Kin, Okinawa}} Kin
{{coord|26|27|19.15|N|127|55|18.50|E|region:JP|display=inline,title|qq=}} || Unranked Shrine |{{nihongo|Seike|聖家|qq=}} |} {{coord_list|section=List of Ryukyu Eight Shrines|qq=}}{{clear|qq=}} == Notes == == References == {{Reflist|qq=}} == Bibliography == * {{Cite book|author=Kenzo Torigoe|title=Studies on Ryūkyū Religious History|publisher=KADOKAWA|year=1965|qq=}} {{Commonscat|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū|qq=}} {{commonscat|Eight Ryukyu Shrines|qq=}} {{Okinawan Shrines|qq=}} {{translated page|ja|琉球八社|version=97200002|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:琉球八社]] [[zh:琉球八社]] {{Commons category|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū}} {{Commons category|Eight Shrines of Ryūkyū}} {{wikidata link|Q11573652}} [[Category:Japanese Enumerations of 8]] [[Category:Ryukyu Kingdom]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
